nativeRange.habitat: Clear, shallow hill streams and small rivers with sandy or rocky substrate; associated with moderate current, dense riparian vegetation, and dappled light in upland drainages of the Irrawaddy and Salween basins

decorPreferences: fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort, Myriophyllum) for cover and spawning substrate
open swimming lanes in the upper water column
some current from a spray bar or gentle filter return
driftwood or smooth stones for naturalistic feel
dark substrate and low-to-moderate lighting to show colour best

diet.notes: Accepts most small prepared foods readily: high-quality micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, and dried daphnia. Benefits from regular supplements of live or frozen micro-foods — daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and cyclops — which support colour and conditioning for breeding. Small mouth limits food particle size; foods should be 0.5–1 mm or smaller.

goodWith: other small peaceful cyprinids (Microdevario, Boraras, Trigonostigma)
small freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — minimal predation risk given small mouth
avoidWith: large or boisterous tankmates that may outcompete at feeding (Zebra Danios, Giant Danios)

imageCandidates.notes: David M. Parichy, 456x196. Sharp lateral view of a live D. nigrofasciatus clearly showing the diagnostic trait: upper horizontal stripe with a lower row of dark spots/dashes (not continuous stripes as in D. rerio). Plain lab background with small species label; best correct-ID sharpness available.

Lerdsuwa, 638x319. Natural planted-tank shot of a live coloured individual showing blue-black flank stripe, orange sub-stripe and spot row. More striking/natural but soft focus and low resolution, so kept as non-recommended extra.
David M. Parichy, 327x136. Second live lab specimen, sharp, same diagnostic stripe-plus-spot-row pattern; smaller near-duplicate of the recommended pick, included as backup.

summary: The Dwarf Danio (Danio nigrofasciatus) is a small, cool-tolerant species from the hill streams of Myanmar, reaching about 4 cm as an adult. It is distinguished from the closely related Zebra Danio by rows of dark spots rather than continuous stripes. Peaceful and active, it does best in groups of eight or more.
careNotes:
The Dwarf Danio (Danio nigrofasciatus) is one of the smallest members of the genus Danio, endemic to upland drainages of Myanmar (Irrawaddy and Salween basins). It reaches approximately 3.5–4 cm total length — noticeably smaller than the widely kept Zebra Danio (D. rerio, 4–5 cm) — and is distinguished by a series of dark spots or short dashes along the flanks rather than the continuous horizontal stripes of D. rerio. The species is sometimes confused in trade with the Pearl Danio (D. albolineatus) and the Glowlight Danio (Celestichthys choprae, whose older name was Danio choprae), all of which are similarly sized and may be sold under overlapping common names.

A key husbandry advantage is cool-water tolerance: the wild habitat in Myanmar hill streams is cooler than most tropical fish require, and D. nigrofasciatus thrives at 20–24°C, making it suitable for unheated aquaria in temperate climates where ambient room temperature falls in this range. Temperatures above 26°C should be avoided, and prolonged exposure above 28°C is harmful.

A group of at least 8 individuals is strongly recommended; larger groups of 12–15 substantially improve schooling cohesion and reduce individual stress. A 60-litre tank with a 60 cm footprint is a workable minimum for a single-species group, though a longer tank (75–80 cm) gives more swimming lane and is preferable. The species occupies the upper water column and is an active, fast-swimming open-water swimmer; surface agitation from a gentle filter return or spray bar is appreciated.

Dwarf Danios belong to the family Danionidae, which was historically grouped within Cyprinidae. Modern molecular research supports recognising Danionidae as a distinct family in its own right, and that classification is now standard in ichthyology.
breedingNotes: The Dwarf Danio is an egg-scattering spawner, broadly similar to other members of the genus. Conditioning pairs or a small group on live and frozen foods — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, and micro-worms — for 1–2 weeks is generally sufficient to trigger spawning. Eggs are typically scattered over fine-leaved plants or a mesh layer above the substrate; adults will consume eggs if left in the same container, so removal after spawning is important. A dedicated breeding tank of 20–40 L with a grid or mesh base, fine-leaved floating plants such as Java moss or hornwort, and a sponge filter running at low flow is the most reliable setup. Eggs hatch in approximately 36–48 hours at 22–24°C; fry become free-swimming 24–48 hours later and accept infusoria, vinegar eels, and paramecia initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms as they grow. Breeding is straightforward once conditioning is understood, but the very small size of newly hatched fry requires care with initial feeding, and a separate breeding setup is needed to protect the eggs — factors that put overall difficulty at moderate.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Danio nigrofasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Myanmar, Irrawaddy/Salween drainages), water parameters (temp 20–24°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm / ~1–10 dH), adult size (~4 cm TL), habitat description (shallow hill streams), diet, and breeding notes. Also confirmed Danionidae family placement and identification notes distinguishing D. nigrofasciatus from D. rerio, D. albolineatus, and Celestichthys choprae.

additional.name: FishBase — Danio nigrofasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), max size (4.0 cm TL), native range (Myanmar), temperature range, and ecology notes. FishBase lists family as Danionidae consistent with modern classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Danio Species Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care overview covering danio genera including D. nigrofasciatus. Confirmed beginner-suitable rating, schooling requirement (6+ recommended; 8+ preferred), feeding on small flake and frozen micro-foods, and cool-water adaptability. Used to calibrate tank size and group-size recommendations.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Danio nigrofasciatus profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist trade source. Confirmed adult size (~3.5 cm), temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.5–7.5), peaceful temperament, schooling behaviour, and compatibility recommendations. Used to cross-check tank size (60 L minimum cited) and minimum group size.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Danionidae taxonomy note
additional.notes: Background reference for Danionidae elevation to family rank from former subfamily Danioninae within Cyprinidae; supports family field value used in this entry. Also cross-referenced against FishBase taxonomy for D. nigrofasciatus which likewise uses Danionidae.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer that spawns over fine-leaved plants or a mesh layer. A separate breeding tank is needed because adults will eat the eggs. Condition the group on live or frozen foods for 1–2 weeks before attempting to breed. Fry are very small and require infusoria or paramecia initially. Eggs hatch in 36–48 hours at 22–24°C.
